summary: Software projects require non-technical work beyond maintenance to succeed, including product management, project management, marketing, sales, and HR. The post defines each role in the context of open source software, explaining how traditional business functions translate to community-driven development. Product management determines project direction and impact, while project management keeps development focused on important issues rather than just interesting ones. Marketing and sales help connect projects with users who need them, and HR addresses the human needs of contributors. The author argues that naming this work explicitly helps communities discuss, promote, and celebrate these essential but often invisible contributions.
key_points: Project management in OSS is uniquely challenging because managers must find non-monetary ways to convince volunteer developers to work on important rather than pleasant tasks
Marketing is more than announcing releases—it requires continuously finding new user bases and the right way to engage them on their level
Good sales is mostly about asking questions and listening, helping institutions understand how software might solve their problems
HR in open source means attending to contributors' wellbeing, managing community tensions, and supporting people through life changes
Many successful projects already have people serving these roles informally, often combined with technical work
money_quotes: Without product management we end up building neat software that may not have as much impact as we would like.
We may choose to work on interesting problems rather than important ones
Project management is particularly hard in OSS because often we don't have clear employer/employee relationships with development teams.
Many excellent software projects go unused because people don't know that the project exists.
Often we can have more impact by telling people about current functionality than by improving or building new functionality.
As developers, we often think of sales and marketing as trying to trick people into buying software. This is only true if your software is bad, and trickery is necessary.
Good sales is mostly about asking questions and listening well.
Software is built by people and people have needs.
key_insight: Open source projects need the same non-technical functions as businesses—product management, project management, marketing, sales, and HR—but adapted to volunteer communities where traditional incentives don't apply.
